is [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") by means of [artificial satellites](/wiki/Artificial_satellites "Artificial satellites")—the measurement of the form and dimensions of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), the location of objects on its surface and the [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth")'s gravity field by means of artificial satellite techniques. It belongs to the broader field of [space geodesy](/wiki/Space_geodesy "Space geodesy"). Traditional [astronomical geodesy](/wiki/Geodetic_astronomy "Geodetic astronomy") is *not* commonly considered a part of satellite geodesy, although there is considerable overlap between the techniques.[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 2

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Measurement of the Earth using satellites

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Wettzell_Laser_Ranging_System.jpg/250px-Wettzell_Laser_Ranging_System.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wettzell_Laser_Ranging_System.jpg)

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Geodesy "Template:Geodesy") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Geodesy "Template talk:Geodesy") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy "Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy") |

**Satellite geodesy** is [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") by means of [artificial satellites](/wiki/Artificial_satellites "Artificial satellites")—the measurement of the form and dimensions of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), the location of objects on its surface and the [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth")'s gravity field by means of artificial satellite techniques. It belongs to the broader field of [space geodesy](/wiki/Space_geodesy "Space geodesy"). Traditional [astronomical geodesy](/wiki/Geodetic_astronomy "Geodetic astronomy") is *not* commonly considered a part of satellite geodesy, although there is considerable overlap between the techniques.[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 2

The main goals of satellite geodesy are:

1. Determination of the figure of the Earth, positioning, and navigation (geometric satellite geodesy)[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 3
2. Determination of [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), [Earth's gravity](/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth "Gravity of Earth") field and its temporal variations (dynamical satellite geodesy[[2]](#cite_note-2) or satellite [physical geodesy](/wiki/Physical_geodesy "Physical geodesy"))
3. Measurement of [geodynamical phenomena](/wiki/Geodynamics "Geodynamics"), such as [crustal dynamics](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") and [polar motion](/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion")[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 4[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 1

Satellite geodetic data and methods can be applied to diverse fields such as [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation"), [hydrography](/wiki/Hydrography "Hydrography"), [oceanography](/wiki/Oceanography "Oceanography") and [geophysics](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics"). Satellite geodesy relies heavily on [orbital mechanics](/wiki/Orbital_mechanics "Orbital mechanics").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=). *(June 2011)* |

### First steps (1957–1970)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First steps (1957–1970)")]

Satellite geodesy began shortly after the launch of [Sputnik](/wiki/Sputnik "Sputnik") in 1957. Observations of [Explorer 1](/wiki/Explorer_1 "Explorer 1") and [Sputnik 2](/wiki/Sputnik_2 "Sputnik 2") in 1958 allowed for an accurate determination of [Earth's flattening](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth").[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 5 The 1960s saw the launch of the Doppler satellite [Transit-1B](/wiki/Transit_(satellite) "Transit (satellite)") and the [balloon satellites](/wiki/Balloon_satellite "Balloon satellite") [Echo 1](/wiki/Project_Echo "Project Echo"), Echo 2, and [PAGEOS](/wiki/PAGEOS "PAGEOS"). The first dedicated geodetic satellite was [ANNA-1B](/wiki/ANNA_1B "ANNA 1B"), a collaborative effort between [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), the [DoD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"), and other civilian agencies.[[3]](#cite_note-GfL-3): 51 ANNA-1B carried the first of the [US Army](/wiki/US_Army "US Army")'s SECOR (Sequential Collation of Range) instruments. These missions led to the accurate determination of the leading [spherical harmonic](/wiki/Spherical_harmonic "Spherical harmonic") coefficients of the geopotential, the general shape of the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), and linked the world's geodetic datums.[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 6

Soviet military satellites undertook geodesic missions to assist in [ICBM](/wiki/ICBM "ICBM") targeting in the late 1960s and early 1970s.

### Toward the World Geodetic System (1970–1990)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Toward the World Geodetic System (1970–1990)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/WORLDWIDE_GEOMETRIC_SATELLITE_TRIANGULATION_NETWORK%2C_BC-4_CAMERAS.GIF/250px-WORLDWIDE_GEOMETRIC_SATELLITE_TRIANGULATION_NETWORK%2C_BC-4_CAMERAS.GIF)](/wiki/File:WORLDWIDE_GEOMETRIC_SATELLITE_TRIANGULATION_NETWORK,_BC-4_CAMERAS.GIF)

Worldwide BC-4 camera geometric satellite triangulation network

The [Transit](/wiki/Transit_(satellite) "Transit (satellite)") satellite system was used extensively for Doppler surveying, navigation, and positioning. Observations of satellites in the 1970s by worldwide triangulation networks allowed for the establishment of the [World Geodetic System](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System"). The development of [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") by the United States in the 1980s allowed for precise navigation and positioning and soon became a standard tool in surveying. In the 1980s and 1990s satellite geodesy began to be used for monitoring of [geodynamic](/wiki/Geodynamics "Geodynamics") phenomena, such as [crustal motion](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"), [Earth rotation](/wiki/Earth_rotation "Earth rotation"), and [polar motion](/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion").

### Modern era (1990–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern era (1990–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/GRACE_artist_concept.jpg/250px-GRACE_artist_concept.jpg)](/wiki/File:GRACE_artist_concept.jpg)

Artist's conception of [GRACE](/wiki/Gravity_Recovery_and_Climate_Experiment "Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment")

The 1990s were focused on the development of permanent geodetic networks and reference frames.[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 7 Dedicated satellites were launched to measure Earth's gravity field in the 2000s, such as [CHAMP](/wiki/CHAMP_(satellite) "CHAMP (satellite)"), [GRACE](/wiki/Gravity_Recovery_and_Climate_Experiment "Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment"), and [GOCE](/wiki/GOCE "GOCE").[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 2

## Measurement techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Measurement techniques")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Jason-1_measurement_system.gif/250px-Jason-1_measurement_system.gif)](/wiki/File:Jason-1_measurement_system.gif)

The Jason-1 measurement system combines major geodetic measurement techniques, including [DORIS](/wiki/DORIS_(geodesy) "DORIS (geodesy)"), [SLR](/wiki/Satellite_laser_ranging "Satellite laser ranging"), [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"), and [altimetry](/wiki/Satellite_altimetry "Satellite altimetry").

Techniques of satellite geodesy may be classified by instrument platform: A satellite may

1. be observed with ground-based instruments (*Earth-to-space-methods*),
2. carry an instrument or sensor as part of its payload to observe the Earth (*space-to-Earth methods*),
3. or use its instruments to track or be tracked by another satellite (*space-to-space methods*).[[1]](#cite_note-Seeber-1): 6

### Earth-to-space methods (satellite tracking)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Earth-to-space methods (satellite tracking)")]

For related topics, see [Satellite tracking (disambiguation)](/wiki/Satellite_tracking_(disambiguation) "Satellite tracking (disambiguation)").

#### Radio techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Radio techniques")]

Main article: [GNSS](/wiki/GNSS "GNSS")

Further information: [Radio navigation](/wiki/Radio_navigation "Radio navigation")

Global navigation satellite systems are dedicated radio positioning services, which can locate a receiver to within a few meters. The most prominent system, [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"), consists of a constellation of 31 satellites (as of December 2013) in high, 12-hour circular orbits, distributed in six planes with 55° [inclinations](/wiki/Orbital_inclination "Orbital inclination"). The principle of location is based on [trilateration](/wiki/Trilateration "Trilateration"). Each satellite transmits a precise [ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris") with information on its own position and a message containing the exact time of transmission. The receiver compares this time of transmission with its own clock at the time of reception and multiplies the difference by the speed of light to obtain a "[pseudorange](/wiki/Pseudorange "Pseudorange")." Four pseudoranges are needed to obtain the precise time and the receiver's position within a few meters. More sophisticated methods, such as [real-time kinematic](/wiki/Real_Time_Kinematic "Real Time Kinematic") (RTK) can yield positions to within a few millimeters.

In geodesy, GNSS is used as an economical tool for [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") and [time transfer](/wiki/Time_transfer "Time transfer").[[4]](#cite_note-gnssgeodesy-4) It is also used for monitoring [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation"), [polar motion](/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion"), and [crustal](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") dynamics.[[4]](#cite_note-gnssgeodesy-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The presence of the GPS signal in space also makes it suitable for [orbit determination](/wiki/Orbit_determination "Orbit determination") and satellite-to-satellite tracking.

*Examples: [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"), [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS"), [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_positioning_system "Galileo positioning system")*

##### Doppler techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Doppler techniques")]

[Doppler](/wiki/Doppler_effect "Doppler effect") positioning involves recording the Doppler shift of a radio signal of stable frequency emitted from a satellite as the satellite approaches and recedes from the observer. The observed frequency depends on the radial velocity of the satellite relative to the observer, which is constrained by [orbital mechanics](/wiki/Orbital_mechanics "Orbital mechanics"). If the observer knows the orbit of the satellite, then recording the Doppler profile determines the observer's position. Conversely, if the observer's position is precisely known, then the orbit of the satellite can be determined and used to study the Earth's gravity. In [DORIS](/wiki/DORIS_(geodesy) "DORIS (geodesy)"), the ground station emits the signal and the satellite receives.

*Examples: [Transit](/wiki/Transit_(satellite) "Transit (satellite)"), [DORIS](/wiki/DORIS_(geodesy) "DORIS (geodesy)"), [Argos](/wiki/Argos_(satellite_system) "Argos (satellite system)")*

#### Optical triangulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Optical triangulation")]

Further information: [Stellar triangulation](/wiki/Stellar_triangulation "Stellar triangulation")

In optical triangulation, the satellite can be used as a very high target for [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation "Triangulation") and can be used to ascertain the geometric relationship between multiple observing stations. Optical triangulation with the BC-4, PC-1000, MOTS, or Baker Nunn cameras consisted of photographic observations of a satellite, or flashing light on the satellite, against a background of stars. The stars, whose positions were accurately determined, provided a framework on the photographic plate or film for a determination of precise directions from camera station to satellite. Geodetic positioning work with cameras was usually performed with one camera observing simultaneously with one or more other cameras. Camera systems are weather dependent and that is one major reason why they fell out of use by the 1980s.[[3]](#cite_note-GfL-3): 51

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/ANNA_1B_track.png/250px-ANNA_1B_track.png)](/wiki/File:ANNA_1B_track.png)

ANNA 1B track on photography taken by [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago") ([Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile")) MOTS station on November 11, 1962

*Examples: [PAGEOS](/wiki/PAGEOS "PAGEOS"), [Project Echo](/wiki/Project_Echo "Project Echo"), [ANNA 1B](/wiki/ANNA_1B "ANNA 1B")*

#### Laser ranging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Laser ranging")]

Main article: [Satellite laser ranging](/wiki/Satellite_laser_ranging "Satellite laser ranging")

In satellite laser ranging (SLR) a global network of observation stations measure the round trip time of flight of ultrashort pulses of [light](/wiki/Light "Light") to satellites equipped with [retroreflectors](/wiki/Retroreflectors "Retroreflectors"). This provides instantaneous range measurements of millimeter level precision which can be accumulated to provide accurate orbit parameters, gravity field parameters (from the orbit perturbations), Earth rotation parameters, tidal Earth's deformations, coordinates and velocities of SLR stations, and other substantial geodetic data. Satellite laser ranging is a proven geodetic technique with significant potential for important contributions to scientific studies of the Earth/Atmosphere/Oceans system. It is the most accurate technique currently available to determine the [geocentric position](/wiki/Geocentric_position "Geocentric position") of an Earth satellite, allowing for the precise calibration of radar [altimeters](/wiki/Altimeter "Altimeter") and separation of long-term instrumentation drift from secular changes in [ocean surface topography](/wiki/Ocean_surface_topography "Ocean surface topography").
Satellite laser ranging contributes to the definition of the international terrestrial reference frames by providing the information about the scale and the origin of the reference frame, the so-called geocenter coordinates.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

*Example: [LAGEOS](/wiki/LAGEOS "LAGEOS")*

### Space-to-Earth methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Space-to-Earth methods")]

#### Altimetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Altimetry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Sealevel_chart.jpg/330px-Sealevel_chart.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sealevel_chart.jpg)

This graph shows the rise in global sea level (in millimeters) measured by the [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")/[CNES](/wiki/CNES "CNES") ocean altimeter mission [TOPEX/Poseidon](/wiki/TOPEX/Poseidon "TOPEX/Poseidon") (on the left) and its follow-on mission [Jason-1](/wiki/Jason-1 "Jason-1"). Image credit: University of Colorado

Satellites such as [Seasat](/wiki/Seasat "Seasat") (1978) and [TOPEX/Poseidon](/wiki/TOPEX/Poseidon "TOPEX/Poseidon") (1992-2006) used advanced dual-band [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") altimeters to measure the height of the Earth's surface (sea, ice, and terrestrial surfaces) from a [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft"). [Jason-1](/wiki/Jason-1 "Jason-1") began in 2001, [Jason-2](/wiki/Jason-2 "Jason-2") in 2008 and [Jason-3](/wiki/Jason-3 "Jason-3") in January 2016.
That measurement, coupled with [orbital](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") elements (possibly augmented by GPS), enables determination of the [terrain](/wiki/Terrain "Terrain"). The two different [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelengths "Wavelengths") of radio waves used permit the altimeter to automatically correct for varying delays in the [ionosphere](/wiki/Ionosphere "Ionosphere").

Spaceborne radar altimeters have proven to be superb tools for mapping [ocean-surface topography](/wiki/Ocean-surface_topography "Ocean-surface topography"), the hills and valleys of the sea surface. These instruments send a microwave pulse to the ocean's surface and record the time it takes to return. A [microwave radiometer](/wiki/Microwave_radiometer "Microwave radiometer") corrects any delay that may be caused by [water vapor](/wiki/Water_vapor "Water vapor") in the [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere"). Other corrections are also required to account for the influence of electrons in the [ionosphere](/wiki/Ionosphere "Ionosphere") and the dry air mass of the atmosphere. Combining these data with the precise location of the spacecraft makes it possible to determine sea-surface height to within a few centimeters (about one inch). The strength and shape of the returning signal also provides information on wind speed and the height of ocean waves. These data are used in ocean models to calculate the speed and direction of [ocean currents](/wiki/Ocean_current "Ocean current") and the amount and location of heat stored in the ocean, which in turn reveals global [climate variations](/wiki/Climate_variation "Climate variation").

##### Laser altimetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Laser altimetry")]

"Satellite laser altimetry" redirects here; not to be confused with [Satellite laser ranging](/wiki/Satellite_laser_ranging "Satellite laser ranging").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=). *(June 2011)* |

A [laser altimeter](/wiki/Laser_altimeter "Laser altimeter") uses the round-trip flight-time of a beam of light at optical or infrared wavelengths to determine the spacecraft's altitude or, conversely, the ground topography.

*Examples: [ICESat](/wiki/ICESat "ICESat"), [MOLA](/wiki/Mars_Orbiter_Laser_Altimeter "Mars Orbiter Laser Altimeter").*

##### Radar altimetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Radar altimetry")]

A [radar altimeter](/wiki/Radar_altimeter "Radar altimeter") uses the round-trip flight-time of a microwave pulse between the satellite and the Earth's surface to determine the distance between the spacecraft and the surface. From this distance or height, the local surface effects such as tides, winds and currents are removed to obtain the satellite height above the geoid. With a precise ephemeris available for the satellite, the geocentric position and ellipsoidal height of the satellite are available for any given observation time. It is then possible to compute the geoid height by subtracting the measured altitude from the ellipsoidal height. This allows direct measurement of the geoid, since the ocean surface closely follows the geoid.[[3]](#cite_note-GfL-3): 64 The difference between the ocean surface and the actual geoid gives [ocean surface topography](/wiki/Ocean_surface_topography "Ocean surface topography").

*Examples: [Seasat](/wiki/Seasat "Seasat"), [Geosat](/wiki/Geosat "Geosat"), [TOPEX/Poseidon](/wiki/TOPEX/Poseidon "TOPEX/Poseidon"), [ERS-1](/wiki/ERS-1 "ERS-1"), [ERS-2](/wiki/ERS-2 "ERS-2"), [Jason-1](/wiki/Jason-1 "Jason-1"), [Jason-2](/wiki/Jason-2 "Jason-2"), [Envisat](/wiki/Envisat "Envisat"), [SWOT (satellite)](/wiki/SWOT_(satellite) "SWOT (satellite)")*

#### Interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR)")]

Main article: [Interferometric synthetic aperture radar](/wiki/Interferometric_synthetic_aperture_radar "Interferometric synthetic aperture radar")

Interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) is a [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") technique used in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") and [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing"). This geodetic method uses two or more [synthetic aperture radar](/wiki/Synthetic_aperture_radar "Synthetic aperture radar") (SAR) images to generate maps of surface deformation or [digital elevation](/wiki/Digital_elevation_map "Digital elevation map"), using differences in the phase of the waves returning to the satellite.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The technique can potentially measure centimetre-scale changes in deformation over timespans of days to
years. It has applications for geophysical monitoring of natural hazards, for example earthquakes, volcanoes and landslides, and also in structural engineering, in particular monitoring of subsidence and structural stability.

*Example: [Seasat](/wiki/Seasat "Seasat"), [TerraSAR-X](/wiki/TerraSAR-X "TerraSAR-X")*

### Space-to-space methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Space-to-space methods")]

#### Gravity gradiometry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Gravity gradiometry")]

Main article: [Gravity gradiometry](/wiki/Gravity_gradiometry "Gravity gradiometry")

A gravity gradiometer can independently determine the components of the gravity vector on a real-time basis. A gravity gradient is simply the spatial derivative of the gravity vector. The gradient can be thought of as the rate of change of a component of the gravity [vector](/wiki/Euclidean_vector "Euclidean vector") as measured over a small distance. Hence, the gradient can be measured by determining the difference in gravity at two close but distinct points. This principle is embodied in several recent moving-base instruments. The gravity gradient at a point is a [tensor](/wiki/Tensor "Tensor"), since it is the derivative of each component of the gravity vector taken in each sensitive axis. Thus, the value of any component of the gravity vector can be known all along the path of the vehicle if gravity gradiometers are included in the system and their outputs are integrated by the system computer. An accurate gravity model will be computed in real-time and a continuous map of normal gravity, elevation, and anomalous gravity will be available.[[3]](#cite_note-GfL-3): 71

*Example: [GOCE](/wiki/GOCE "GOCE")*

#### Satellite-to-satellite tracking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_geodesy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Satellite-to-satellite tracking")]

This technique uses satellites to track other satellites. There are a number of variations which may be used for specific purposes such as [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") field investigations and [orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") improvement.

* A [high altitude satellite](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") may act as a relay from ground tracking stations to a [low altitude satellite](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit"). In this way, low altitude satellites may be observed when they are not accessible to ground stations. In this type of tracking, a signal generated by a tracking station is received by the relay satellite and then retransmitted to a lower altitude satellite. This signal is then returned to the ground station by the same path.
* Two low altitude satellites can track one another observing mutual orbital variations caused by gravity field irregularities. A prime example of this is [GRACE](/wiki/Gravity_Recovery_and_Climate_Experiment "Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment").
* Several high altitude satellites with accurately known orbits, such as [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") satellites, may be used to fix the position of a low altitude satellite.

These examples present a few of the possibilities for the application of satellite-to-satellite tracking. Satellite-to-satellite tracking data was first collected and analyzed in a high-low configuration between [ATS-6](/wiki/ATS-6 "ATS-6") and [GEOS-3](/wiki/GEOS_(satellite) "GEOS (satellite)"). The data was studied to evaluate its potential for both orbit and gravitational model refinement.[[3]](#cite_note-GfL-3): 68
     *Example: [GRACE](/wiki/Gravity_Recovery_and_Climate_Experiment "Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment")*
